{"step_by_step_thinking": "A chemotactic cytokine is one that directs the movement of cells, particularly immune cells, to a site of inflammation. Among the options, IL-8 (interleukin-8) is specifically described as a chemotactic cytokine in multiple documents. For example, Document [14] states that IL-8 'causes neutrophil chemotaxis', and Document [16] notes that IL-8 and granulocyte colony-stimulating factor promote neutrophil chemotaxis and maturation. Document [23] also confirms that IL-8 causes chemotaxis and neutrophil maturation. While IL-1, IL-6, and TNF are pro-inflammatory cytokines and can influence immune responses, they are not primarily described as chemotactic. IL-1 and TNF are more involved in activating immune cells and promoting inflammation, whereas IL-8 is explicitly linked to directing cell migration. Therefore, IL-8 is the most accurate answer as a chemotactic cytokine.", "answer_choice": "C"}
